analogy: Think of "Electricity" through the chapter's own opening images: Magnetic Effects of Electric Current 12 CHAPTER In the previous Chapter on ‘Electricity’ we learnt about the heating effects of electric current. What could be the other effects of electric current? We know that an electric current-carrying wire behaves like a magnet. Let us perform... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Electricity is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that magnetic Effects of Electric Current 12 CHAPTER In the previous Chapter on ‘Electricity’ we learnt about the heating effects of electric current.

The text explains that what could be the other effects of electric current?

The text explains that we know that an electric current-carrying wire behaves like a magnet.

The text explains that let us perform the following Activity to reinforce it.

The text explains that the wire XY is kept perpendicular to the plane of paper. n Horizontally place a small compass near to this copper wire.

The text explains that see the position of its needle. n Pass the current through the circuit by inserting the key into the plug. n Observe the change in the position of the compass needle.

The text explains that figure 12.1 Figure 12.1 Figure 12.1 Figure 12.1 Figure 12.1 Compass needle is deflected on passing an electric current through a metallic conductor We see that the needle is deflected.

The text explains that it means that the electric current through the copper wire has produced a magnetic effect.

The text explains that thus we can say that electricity and magnetism are linked to each other.

The text explains that then, what about the reverse possibility of an electric effect of moving magnets?

The text explains that in this Chapter we will study magnetic fields and such electromagnetic effects.

The text explains that we shall also study about electromagnets which involve the magnetic effect of electric current.

The text explains that hans Christian Oersted (1777–1851) Hans Christian Oersted, one of the leading scientists of the 19th century, played a crucial role in understanding electromagnetism.

The text explains that in 1820 he accidentally discovered that a compass needle got deflected when an electric current passed through a metallic wire placed nearby.

The text explains that through this observation Oersted showed that electricity and magnetism were related phenomena.

The text explains that his research later created technologies such as the radio, television and fiber optics.

The text explains that the ends of the compass needle point approximately towards north and south directions.

The text explains that the north pole of the compass is directed away from the north pole of the magnet.

The text explains that magnetic Effects of Electric Current 197 Magnetic field is a quantity that has both direction and magnitude.

The text explains that the direction of the magnetic field is taken to be the direction in which a north pole of the compass needle moves inside it.

The text explains that inside the magnet, the direction of field lines is from its south pole to its north pole.

The text explains that if they did, it would mean that at the point of intersection, the compass needle would point towards two directions, which is not possible.

The text explains that in order to find the direction of the field produced let us repeat the activity in the following way – Figure 12.3 Figure 12.3 Figure 12.3 Figure 12.3 Figure 12.3 Drawing a magnetic field line with the help of a compass needle n Mark the position of two ends of the needle. n Now move the needle to a new position such that its south pole occupies the position previously occupied by its north pole. n In this way, proceed step by step till you reach the south pole of the magnet as shown in Fig.

The text explains that 12.5 (a). n Place the straight wire parallel to and over a compass needle. n Plug the key in the circuit. n Observe the direction of deflection of the north pole of the needle.

The text explains that this would result in the change of the direction of current through the copper wire, that is, from south to north. n Observe the change in the direction of deflection of the needle.

The text explains that you will see that now the needle moves in opposite direction, that is, towards the west [Fig.

The text explains that it means that the direction of magnetic field produced by the electric current is also reversed.

The text explains that the deflection in the needle becomes opposite when the direction of the current is reversed.

The text explains that 12.2.2 Right-Hand Thumb Rule A convenient way of finding the direction of magnetic field associated with a current-carrying conductor is given in Fig.

The text explains that they represent the magnetic field lines. n How can the direction of the magnetic field be found?

The text explains that the direction of the north pole of the compass needle would give the direction of the field lines produced by the electric current through the straight wire at point P.

The text explains that show the direction by an arrow. n Does the direction of magnetic field lines get reversed if the direction of current through the straight copper wire is reversed?

The text explains that the arrows in the circles show the direction of the field lines.

The text explains that science 200 Imagine that you are holding a current-carrying straight conductor in your right hand such that the thumb points towards the direction of current.

The text explains that then your fingers will wrap around the conductor in the direction of the field lines of the magnetic field, as shown in Fig.

The text explains that example 12.1 A current through a horizontal power line flows in east to west direction.

The text explains that what is the direction of magnetic field at a point directly below it and at a point directly above it?

The text explains that solution The current is in the east-west direction.

The text explains that if we consider ourselves driving a corkscrew in the direction of the current, then the direction of the rotation of corkscrew is the direction of the magnetic field.

The text explains that by applying the right hand rule, it is easy to check that every section of the wire contributes to the magnetic field lines in the same direction within the loop.

The text explains that magnetic Effects of Electric Current 201 We know that the magnetic field produced by a current-carrying wire at a given point depends directly on the current passing through it.

The text explains that this is because the current in each circular turn has the same direction, and the field due to each turn then just adds up.

The text explains that apply the right-hand rule to find out the direction of the magnetic field inside and outside the loop.

The text explains that 12.12. n Place a strong horse-shoe magnet in such a way that the rod lies between the two poles with the magnetic field directed upwards.

The text explains that you will notice that the rod gets displaced. n Reverse the direction of current flowing through the rod and observe the direction of its displacement.

The text explains that it also suggests that the direction of force is also reversed when the direction of current through the conductor is reversed.

The text explains that now change the direction of field to vertically downwards by interchanging the two poles of the magnet.

The text explains that magnetic Effects of Electric Current 203 the direction of force acting on the current-carrying rod gets reversed.

The text explains that it shows that the direction of the force on the conductor depends upon the direction of current and the direction of the magnetic field.

The text explains that experiments have shown that the displacement of the rod is largest (or the magnitude of the force is the highest) when the direction of current is at right angles to the direction of the magnetic field.

The text explains that in such a condition we can use a simple rule to find the direction of the force on the conductor.

The text explains that in Activity 12.7, we considered the direction of the current and that of the magnetic field perpendicular to each other and found that the force is perpendicular to both of them.

Source: NCERT Class 10 Science, Chapter 12: Electricity

Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
